children.name: Pluvialis
children.description: Large upright plovers of open habitats. The mournful whistle of the Golden Plover is the defining sound of Swedish upland mires and heaths; autumn flocks of tens of thousands gather on coastal farmland.

children.name: Pluvialis apricaria
children.description: Its plaintive whistling song carries across upland mires and bogs where it breeds in northern Sweden. Huge flocks gather on coastal farmland on migration, the spangled gold-and-black upperparts giving the bird its name.

children.name: Pluvialis squatarola
children.description: A larger, greyer cousin of the Golden Plover, breeding on high-Arctic tundra and passing through Sweden on migration. In flight it shows diagnostic black 'armpits' against white underwings.

children.name: Pluvialis fulva
children.description: Breeds on Siberian and Alaskan tundra and winters across the Pacific and southern Asia; slimmer and longer-legged than its European cousin, with remarkable long-distance migrations to oceanic islands.

children.name: Pluvialis dominica
children.description: Makes one of the longest migrations of any shorebird, breeding in Arctic North America and wintering on the pampas of southern South America, often flying non-stop over the Atlantic.

children.name: Vanellus
children.description: Lapwings with broad rounded wings and elaborate crests.

children.name: Vanellus vanellus
children.description: The tofsvipa, with its wispy crest, iridescent green back, and tumbling display flight, is a beloved harbinger of spring on Swedish farmland and wet meadows. Its plaintive 'pee-wit' call gives it many of its folk names.

children.name: Vanellus spinosus
children.description: A boldly black-white-and-brown lapwing of African and Middle Eastern wetlands, armed with a sharp carpal spur on each wing used in defence of its nest.

children.name: Vanellus miles
children.description: A large, noisy lapwing of Australia and New Zealand with conspicuous yellow facial wattles; famous for aggressively dive-bombing intruders near its ground nest.

children.name: Vanellus coronatus
children.description: A common and adaptable lapwing of African grasslands and overgrazed land, named for the black cap encircled by a white halo.

children.name: Vanellus gregarius
children.description: A Critically Endangered lapwing breeding on the Central Asian steppes; populations crashed dramatically through the 20th century, prompting intensive international conservation efforts.

children.name: Vanellus indicus
children.description: A familiar South Asian lapwing whose loud 'did-he-do-it' call rings across farmland and wetlands; readily nests on open ground including rooftops.

children.name: Vanellus armatus
children.description: A striking black, white and grey lapwing of southern and eastern African wetlands, named for its metallic 'tink-tink' alarm call like a hammer on an anvil.

children.name: Vanellus chilensis
children.description: The most widespread lapwing of South America and the national bird of Uruguay; a noisy, aggressive defender of its grassland and wetland nest sites.

children.name: Charadrius
children.description: Small fast-running waders of sandy shores and gravel bars.

children.name: Charadrius hiaticula
children.description: A neat, fast-running little plover of sandy and shingly shores, distinguished by a complete black breast-band and orange legs. Breeds on Swedish coasts and Baltic islands and uses a broken-wing display to lure predators from the nest.

children.name: Charadrius dubius
children.description: A slighter, more freshwater-loving plover than the Ringed Plover, with a distinctive yellow eye-ring and no white wing-bar. It nests on gravel pits, riverbanks, and industrial ground across southern Sweden.

children.name: Charadrius morinellus
children.description: A confiding mountain plover that breeds on the high fell tops of Swedish Lapland, where the larger, brighter female courts the male and leaves him to incubate. On migration small 'trips' pause on traditional ploughed-field staging sites.

children.name: Charadrius alexandrinus
children.description: A pale sand-coloured plover of coastal beaches and saline lagoons across the Old World; declining in Europe due to disturbance of nesting beaches.

children.name: Charadrius vociferus
children.description: A noisy double-banded plover of North American fields and gravel; famous for its broken-wing distraction display to lure predators away from the nest.

children.name: Charadrius melodus
children.description: A pale, Near Threatened beach-nesting plover of North American shores and the Great Plains, the focus of major habitat-protection programmes.

children.name: Charadrius wilsonia
children.description: A stocky plover with a heavy black bill, found on sandy and muddy coasts of the Americas; specialises in catching fiddler crabs.

children.name: Charadrius veredus
children.description: A long-legged plover breeding on the dry grasslands of Mongolia and China and migrating to northern Australia.

children.name: Charadrius montanus
children.description: A plover of short-grass prairie and bare ground in western North America rather than shorelines; declining with the loss of native grassland and prairie dog colonies.

children.name: Charadrius bicinctus
children.description: Breeds in New Zealand on braided riverbeds and uniquely migrates west to winter in Australia; marked by two contrasting breast bands.

children.name: Charadrius pecuarius
children.description: A small, well-camouflaged African plover that buries its eggs in sand when leaving the nest; widespread on inland and coastal flats.

children.name: Charadrius obscurus
children.description: A stocky, rufous-breasted plover endemic to New Zealand; one subspecies is Critically Endangered with intensive nest protection underway.

children.name: Anarhynchus
children.description: A largely Southern Hemisphere genus including the unique wrybill and several sand plovers.

children.name: Anarhynchus frontalis
children.description: A New Zealand endemic and the only bird in the world with a bill bent sideways (always to the right), used to sweep under riverbed stones for insect larvae; Near Threatened.

children.name: Anarhynchus leschenaultii
children.description: Breeds on Central Asian deserts and migrates to coasts of Africa, Asia and Australia; a long-billed sand plover of tidal flats.

children.name: Anarhynchus mongolus
children.description: A small migratory sand plover breeding in the mountains and tundra of eastern Siberia, wintering on Indo-Pacific shores.

children.name: Oreopholus
children.description: A monotypic genus of high-altitude South American plover.

children.name: Oreopholus ruficollis
children.description: A handsome plover of the high Andes and Patagonian steppe, distinguished by a rich tawny throat and bold black belly patch.
